4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every engineering team wants confidence before releasing software.
The problem is that confidence is difficult to measure.
Because of that, many organizations rely on metrics that appear objective, and one of the most common is test coverage.
“We have 90% coverage.”
“We’re aiming for 100%.”
“Our coverage dropped after the last release.”
At first glance, these statements sound reassuring.
Unfortunately, they often create a false sense of security.
After working with software quality across financial services, enterprise systems, and SaaS products, I’ve learned that high coverage does not necessarily mean high quality.
Sometimes it means exactly the opposite.
The Comfort of a Single Number
Engineering leaders naturally look for measurable indicators.
Coverage is attractive because it’s easy to calculate.
Modern tools display colorful dashboards showing exactly which lines of code were executed during automated tests.
The problem is that executing code is not the same as validating behavior.
A line of code can be executed without verifying whether it produced the correct outcome.
You can reach 95% coverage while still missing the bugs that matter most to users.
Coverage tells you what your tests touched.
It does not tell you whether your software is actually reliable.
Those are completely different questions.
What Coverage Actually Measures
Coverage tools generally answer questions like:

• Which lines of code were executed?

• Which methods were called?

• Which branches were visited?
Notice what they don’t answer.
They don’t tell you whether:

• business rules are correct;

• user workflows work as expected;

• APIs behave correctly under unexpected inputs;

• integrations continue working after deployment;

• the application is usable;

• critical customer journeys are protected.
Coverage measures execution.
Quality measures confidence.
Those are not interchangeable.
The Illusion of High Coverage
I’ve seen projects reporting over 90% code coverage while production incidents continued to happen regularly.
Why?
Because the tests were validating implementation details instead of business behavior.
Developers naturally tend to test the code they just wrote.
That usually produces many small unit tests with excellent coverage numbers.
But users don’t interact with isolated methods.
They interact with complete workflows.
A customer doesn’t care whether an internal service has 100% coverage.
They care whether they can successfully place an order, complete a payment, recover a password or generate a report.
Software succeeds or fails at the workflow level.
Not at the method level.
Confidence Is Built Around Risk
Instead of asking:
“How much code is covered?”
I prefer asking:
“What could seriously impact the business if this fails?”
That simple question changes the entire testing strategy.
Not every piece of code deserves the same investment.
Some components are low risk.
Others can stop an entire business operation.
A login flow.
A payment process.
Authentication.
Customer onboarding.
Data synchronization.
These deserve significantly more attention than an internal utility function.
Quality Engineering is fundamentally a process of risk management.
Coverage percentages rarely reflect that reality.
A Better Way to Think About Testing
Rather than optimizing for coverage, I encourage engineering teams to optimize for confidence.
That usually means combining different testing strategies.
For example:

• unit tests validate isolated business logic;

• integration tests validate system interactions;

• API tests protect contracts;

• end-to-end tests protect critical customer journeys;

• exploratory testing uncovers unexpected behaviors;

• performance testing validates scalability;

• security testing reduces exposure to common vulnerabilities.
Each testing layer answers different questions.
Together, they create confidence.
No single metric can replace that.
Why More Tests Can Make Things Worse
Another misconception is that writing more tests automatically improves quality.
It doesn’t.
Poorly designed automation creates maintenance costs.
Flaky tests reduce trust.
Slow pipelines delay feedback.
Duplicated scenarios increase complexity.
Eventually, engineers begin ignoring failures because they no longer trust the test suite.
At that point, automation becomes technical debt instead of an engineering asset.
The objective isn’t to maximize the number of tests.
The objective is to maximize the value each test provides.
The Role of Artificial Intelligence
Artificial Intelligence introduces an interesting shift.
Many discussions focus on AI generating test cases automatically.
That’s useful.
But I believe the bigger opportunity lies elsewhere.
AI can help engineering teams make better testing decisions.
Instead of simply creating more tests, AI can assist by:

• identifying risky code changes;

• suggesting areas that deserve deeper validation;

• detecting historical defect patterns;

• prioritizing regression suites;

• analyzing failures faster;

• recommending additional scenarios based on previous incidents.
This changes the conversation.
The goal is no longer producing more automation.
The goal is making automation smarter.
That distinction matters.
Engineering Owns Quality
One belief has remained constant throughout my career.
Quality is not the responsibility of the QA team.
It’s the responsibility of engineering.
QA engineers contribute expertise.
Automation engineers build confidence.
Developers design maintainable software.
Product teams define expected behavior.
Everyone contributes to quality.
When organizations treat quality as a shared engineering responsibility, testing stops becoming a phase at the end of development.
It becomes part of how software is built.
That’s where the biggest improvements happen.
Measuring What Actually Matters
If coverage isn’t the primary KPI, what should engineering leaders monitor?
I find these questions much more valuable:

• How quickly do we detect failures?

• How often do production incidents occur?

• How reliable are our deployments?

• How much confidence do engineers have before releasing?

• Which business workflows remain unprotected?

• Where are our highest engineering risks?
These questions lead to better decisions than simply tracking a percentage on a dashboard.
Because software quality isn’t about maximizing metrics.
It’s about minimizing uncertainty.
Final Thoughts
Coverage is useful.
I still monitor it.
I still include it in engineering dashboards.
But I never confuse it with software quality.
Coverage is an indicator.
Not an objective.
Engineering teams that chase coverage percentages often optimize for numbers.
Engineering teams that optimize for confidence build better software.
And in the long run, confidence is far more valuable than coverage.
